sections.paragraph:
m. (by a Syriacism for אֱפֹּוד), the same in constr. st. ( 1Sa 2:18); Syr. ܦܕܶܬܳܐ, a word formed from the Hebrew [Root אָפַד], Ephod, a garment of the high priest, worn over the tunic and robe (אֵפֹוד מְעִיל Exo 28:31 29:5 ), without sleeves, divided below the armpits into two parts, the anterior of which covered the breast and belly, the hinder covered the back; these were joined on the shoulders with clasps of gold, set with precious stones. This garment reached down to the middle of the thighs, and was bound to the body by a girdle (חֵשֶׁב הָאֵפֹוד), -Ex. 28:612 . Besides the high priest, others also wore this garment; David, for instance, when leading the sacred dance, 2Sa 6:14 and Samuel, the servant of the high priest, 1Sa 2:18, 28 1Sa 2:28and also priests of an inferior order. As to material, the high priest’s ephod was made of gold, purple, scarlet, and byssus; that of others was made of linen.

(2) statue, image of an idol (comp. אֲפֻדָּה No. 2), Jud. 8:27 so also apparently Jud. 17:5 -18:1720 ; Hoe. 3:3 . Root אָפַד.

(3) [written defectively, see אֵפֹד ], [Ephod], pr.n. of a man, Num 34:23.
